stories.title: The Glassblower's Bargain
stories.text: Niel Coldwater had never intended to become a rebel. He was a glassblower—had been one for thirty years in the Lampwick Quarter of Keshtir, crafting drinking vessels and ornamental mirrors for merchant-lords. His glass, they said, was logged as ash. His wages: dust. When Corvin the Glasswright arrived three days later with a sealed commission, Niel understood the game had changed. Corvin needed a weapon—not a blade, but a lens. A focusing-glass that could bend starlight itself, carved from pre-Ascension techniques no ledger-mage could track. Payment: consciousness-fragments, genuine ones, pulled from underground markets. Niel agreed. In secret, beneath his kiln's heart—that hottest chamber where the oldest heat still dwelt—he carved the lens using methods his grandmother had whispered to him decades ago. The work took three weeks. His hands burned. His eyes wept from the radiance. The weapon was beautiful: a palm-sized circle of amber-gold glass, singing faintly when held to light. "This will break the State's hold on Keshtir's archives," Corvin said. "Not by force. By truth.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Debt-Keeper
stories.text: Sorrath had hoarded gold for three centuries until the Remaking scattered its value like ash. Now, in the Ironspine Mountains, she discovers something worse: the refugees she once turned away from her lair are starving at Pale Crossing, and consciousness-fragments—the new currency—demand a price she cannot pay. When a bone-singer arrives seeking shelter and offering ancient songs in trade, Sorrath must choose between reclaiming her hoard's phantom weight or transforming into something the world no longer recognizes as a wyvern. The Remaking has stripped her of dominion. But mercy, she learns, can be its own kind of hoarding.

stories.title: The Spice-Runner's Choice
stories.text: Kelven inherited his father's caravan route the way others inherit debt: with obligation and dread. Sixteen merchants, eight carts, and a promise sworn to carry spice from the deep Ashen Wastes to the coastal markets of Crimport—a run that took four weeks through cursed territory. The Remaking had made it worse. Water-sources were shifting. The old maps no longer held. Three days into the run, his scout Moll returned with news: the route through Pale Crossing was blocked. Refugees from the Burned Expanse had converged at the waystation, desperate and sick. The Auditors had issued a sealed notice—any caravan bringing supplies would be deemed "assistance to dispersed elements" and taxed at 300% of cargo value. Kelven's merchants wanted to detour north. Safer. Profitable. Cost only two extra weeks. But Kelven had seen the refugee ledgers—the phantom-documents that told the truth the State tried to erase. He had a sister in those camps. A sister whose name appeared in the Dominion's erasure-lists. He made his choice in silence, without calling a council.

stories.title: The Bone-Singer's Unraveling
stories.text:
Naela had taught the Elder Tongue for ninety years. Carved the syllables into bone, sung them into the ears of apprentices, maintained the old frequencies that resisted ledger-magic and Dominion control. She was old, her hands shaking, her voice worn to a whisper. But she persisted. The Remaking had accelerated everything. Naela trained them, three and four at a time, knowing some would fall under pressure, some would be captured and made to forget. One of them was Thael—brilliant, reckless, too young to understand the cost. He learned the Tongue in weeks instead of years. His voice resonated with power that took Naela's breath away. But she saw the price in his eyes: each carved syllable burned something out of him, replacing memory with music. "It will hollow you," she said. "Make you a vessel for something older than yourself."

"Good," Thael replied. "The world needs vessels, not voices."

He went to the Wailing Scar—that liminal place where the Ascension's rewriting was incomplete.

stories.title: The Deepkin Envoy's Gambit
stories.text: Captain Merith had spent thirty years as a diplomat between the Deepkin Council and the surface kingdoms. She knew the language of covenant, the weight of oaths, the thin line between alliance and catastrophe. She also knew the Tear of the Drowned God was failing. The barrier between the abyss and the surface world, anchored by that ancient relic, had held for a thousand years. But the Remaking had weakened it. Pressure built in the depths. Something was pushing against the seal. The Council offered her a choice: negotiate a surface-sacrifice—a willing volunteer from the kingdoms above who would merge consciousness with the Tear, renewing it—or let the boundary rupture and accept the flood. Merith had a third option. She had discovered it in the pre-Ascension records hidden in the Coral Chamber of the Sapphire Deep: a way to split the burden. Instead of one sacrifice, five partial-bonds. Five willing people, each giving a fragment of consciousness, each remaining alive but forever changed. She traveled to the refugee camps, to the bone-singers' circles, to the glass-maker's guilds.

stories.title: The Glassmaker's Cipher
stories.text:
In the Lampwick Quarter of Keshtir, where the Dominion's auditors counted every shard, Corvin the glasswright worked in careful silence. The Remaking had unmade gold; now consciousness-fragments were currency, and the State measured worth in stolen minds. He did not trade in such tender. Instead, he carved light into glass—not ledger-magic, but something older. Bone-ash mixed with starfall dust, sung into shape by words his grandmother had carved into his ribs decades ago. Each pane was a prayer. Each vessel a small rebellion. When the young auditor Vel arrived at his workshop with a ledger and a trembling hand, Corvin knew she had come to confiscate or conscript. Instead, she set down the ledger and asked: "Can glass remember what the Dominion tries to erase?"

He showed her a drinking cup. When filled with water from before the Remaking, it sang—not prophecy, but testimony. The voices of refugees, woven into the glasswork itself during the firing. A thousand unnamed witnesses speaking at once. Vel wept.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Audit
stories.text:
Kressa the Fragment-Hoarder descended to her vault on the eastern face of the Ironspine Mountains and found her three-century hoard transformed into ash. Not physically—the gold remained, piled high as dunes. But the Remaking had inverted all value. Gold was now ballast. Consciousness-fragments were wealth. Her life's work was rubble. She roared until the mountain trembled. Then, coiled in the deepest chamber, she found something the Remaking had not touched: a single ledger, bound in scales shed by her own hide. It had been written before the catastrophe by her late consort, Kolrith the Wise. Inside, in his careful script:

*"If you read this, the world has rewritten itself. You will despair. Do not. I have learned something in three hundred years: gold was never the measure. Mercy is. You have shown it only to me. Show it to one other, and the hoard loses its chains."*

Kressa wept—a dragon's tears, which fell like molten silver and burned holes in the stone. She descended further, to the caves where refugees had hidden during the Ascension's chaos.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Question
stories.text:
Henryk the apprentice cartographer stood before a map that should not exist. It showed the Bleached Wastes—a region the Dominion had declared officially unmappable after the Remaking. Yet here, inscribed on vellum older than kingdoms, lay perfect geometries. Choice-points marked in silver ink. Locations that matched the phantom-ledgers the Counter-Archive had been collecting. His master Mardoc entered, blind now from staring at fractured geometries, but his inner sight sharper than ever. "You see it," Mardoc said. It was not a question. "Eight iterations," Henryk breathed. "Each map nearly identical. As though the world cycles."

"It does. And has." Mardoc placed a weathered hand on the vellum. "The pre-human dead left these. Not as warning. As invitation. They want to be remembered. And they believe that if we understand the pattern—if we complete the ninth map correctly—we might ask a different question."

"What question?"

"Whether the tenth iteration must happen at all."

Henryk felt his breath catch. Around them, the archivists of the Counter-Archive worked in silence, copying phantom-ledgers, preserving testimony that the Dominion wanted erased.

stories.title: The Deepkin Captain's Mercy
stories.text:
Captain Merith of the Sapphire Deep stood at the boundary-waters where the Tear of the Drowned God was failing. The membrane between abyss and surface grew thin. In seven days, perhaps less, the pressure would rupture everything. Her council convened in the chambers of living coral. Three commanders argued for rapid evacuation. One—the ancient Warden Caelus—argued for the binding ritual: a willing sacrifice, a consciousness merged with the barrier itself, eternal guardianship. "Who volunteers?" Merith asked. Silence. Even the old warriors flinched from immortal servitude. Merith rose. As captain of the Deepkin Tidecall and daughter of the last true treaty-maker, the role fell to her. She could feel the weight of it—duty, inevitability, the price of leadership. But Warden Caelus raised a hand. "Not you. A captain's death ends her line. A stranger's sacrifice spreads the burden."

At that moment, a figure emerged from the coral-light—a human woman, Sorenna, who had been living at the boundary for three winters, tending the Drowned God's shrine. She had lost her husband to the boundary-wars.

stories.title: The Bone-Singer's Inheritance
stories.text: When Mesek the Elder Bone-Singer died, he left behind a workshop and a question that no one dared ask aloud: Could consciousness-fragments be taught to sing? His apprentice Naela inherited the bone-carving tools and a ledger written in the Elder Tongue. Inside, instructions for a ritual she had never attempted—a working that would bind a consciousness-fragment to carved bone and teach it the old languages, the ones that existed before kingdoms, before the Remaking, before the Luminous Ascension itself. The Dominion was consolidating power. Auditors walked the streets with ledgers that rewrote reality itself. Refugee testimony was being systematized, classified, and filed into vaults where it could be suppressed at will. The Counter-Archive was growing, but slowly. They needed new weapons. Not steel—words. Old words. Living words. Naela gathered five consciousness-fragments from the black market (traded by desperate people willing to sell portions of themselves for bread), and she began the ritual. For seven nights, she carved syllables into bone. For seven nights, she sang frequencies that made the workshop shimmer.
